name: Houston Independent School District
districtFact: HISD's magnet school application window opens each fall — typically November through January — for the following school year. Families who arrive in Houston after the window closes cannot access specialty programs until the next cycle. For a district where STEM, fine arts, IB, and dual-language magnet tracks carry enrollment caps and waitlists, a move timed two months earlier can open a year's worth of school choice options that would otherwise be unavailable.

about.title: About Houston Independent School District
about.content:
Houston Independent School District was founded in 1877 and today serves approximately 194,000 students across more than 275 schools — making it the largest school district in Texas and one of the ten largest in the United States. The district covers the City of Houston proper, a 330-square-mile territory that includes neighborhoods as economically and ethnically distinct as River Oaks, the East End, Acres Homes, and Midtown.

HISD's student population speaks more than 60 languages at home. Spanish is the most common home language after English, followed by Arabic, Vietnamese, Urdu, and Mandarin — a linguistic profile that reflects Houston's status as one of the most ethnically diverse large cities in the country. Approximately 75% of HISD students qualify for free or reduced-price lunch, a figure that shapes the district's federal funding profile and its emphasis on magnet programs as a school-choice mechanism within the district's own boundaries.

In March 2023, the Texas Education Agency placed HISD under state conservatorship following years of academic performance concerns at Wheatley High School. The state superintendent installed by TEA has restructured several district-level departments and made changes to school leadership. Families relocating to Houston should be aware that administrative structures and some program offerings may evolve as the conservatorship continues — checking houstonisd.org for current enrollment and program information before making school decisions is particularly important during this period.

about.title: Magnet Schools and School Choice
about.content:
HISD operates one of the largest magnet school networks in the United States, with specialized programs in STEM, fine and performing arts, International Baccalaureate (IB), dual-language immersion, law and government, and early college. Magnet programs are open to all Houston residents regardless of attendance zone — but enrollment is capped, popular programs have waitlists, and the application window opens each fall, typically November through January, for the following school year.

Families who relocate to Houston after the magnet application window closes cannot apply for specialty programs until the next fall cycle. A child who arrives in February will be placed in their neighborhood school for the current year and must wait until the following November to apply for magnet programs starting the year after. For families with a specific STEM, arts, or IB track in mind, timing a move to arrive before November is worth planning for — the difference between a November arrival and a February arrival can represent a 20-month delay before a child can access their target program.

HISD's magnet programs are housed at schools across the city, not concentrated in one area. Carnegie Vanguard High School (STEM/pre-medicine), HSPVA (fine arts), DeBakey High School for Health Professions, and Eastwood Academy are among the programs with the most competitive admissions. The district's School Choice office manages applications at houstonisd.org/schoolchoice — families should contact that office directly for current program availability, as offerings can change year to year.

about.title: Before You Move to HISD
about.content:
Four things families should do before committing to a Houston address.

Verify your school assignment by exact address. HISD attendance zones are drawn at the street level and follow Houston's patchwork of municipal annexations, deed restrictions, and neighborhood boundaries. Two houses on the same block can fall in different elementary zones. Use the HISD school locator at houstonisd.org to confirm your assigned schools before signing a lease or mortgage.

Check the magnet application timeline before your move date. The school choice window typically opens in November and closes in January for the following school year. If you arrive in Houston after January, your child will be placed in the neighborhood school for their grade and cannot enter the magnet lottery until next fall. Families targeting Carnegie Vanguard, HSPVA, or DeBakey should time their move to allow a fall application.

Prepare for an early August school start. HISD typically begins the school year in the first or second week of August — earlier than most school districts in the Northeast, Midwest, and West Coast. Summer camps, childcare, and travel plans that assume a post-Labor Day start will not work in Houston. Update childcare arrangements and summer programming accordingly.

Build a hurricane contingency plan before August. Atlantic hurricane season runs June through November, and peak season (August–October) overlaps directly with the start of school. Harris County flooding can close HISD schools independently of direct hurricane landfalls — the memory of Harvey (2017) and Beryl (2024) is recent. Register for HISD's emergency notification system at houstonisd.org and review your specific school's emergency procedures during summer orientation.

content: Houston ISD Spring Break (Mar 8–12) is among the earliest in Texas. South Padre Island and Cancún pricing spikes quickly once Houston schools release — book at least 6–8 weeks out for coastal travel.
Harris County suburban districts (Katy ISD, Cy-Fair, Spring ISD) may have different Spring Break dates. Verify each district's calendar separately if children are enrolled in multiple districts.
Hurricane season runs June–November. HISD builds makeup days into the calendar but may extend the school year if closures exceed state thresholds. Book late-May travel with flexibility.

Magnet school applications open in fall — contact the HISD School Choice office by October. The most popular STEM and IB programs fill quickly, and missing the application window means waiting a full year.

districtFaqs.q: Does Houston ISD have makeup days for hurricanes or tropical storms?
districtFaqs.a: HISD builds emergency makeup days into its calendar each year, and Texas Education Agency rules allow districts to apply for weather-related waivers that substitute instructional minutes for missed calendar days. As a result, the school year does not always extend after weather closures — HISD may absorb lost days through waivers rather than adding calendar days at the end. However, if closures exceed the buffer, the district may extend the year past the published last day. Families booking late-May travel should check HISD's official communications if a storm has caused extended school closures during the year. Register for emergency notifications at houstonisd.org.

districtFaqs.q: How is the HISD school year organized — semesters or quarters?
districtFaqs.a: HISD organizes its school year into two semesters. The fall semester runs from the August start through winter break, with finals typically held in December before the holiday. The spring semester runs from January through the May end of year, with final exams in May. HISD does not use a four-quarter report card system — grading cycles may vary by school level, so check with your specific campus for grading period dates and report card release schedules.

districtFaqs.q: When do HISD magnet school applications open, and how do I apply?
districtFaqs.a: HISD magnet school applications typically open in November and close in January for admission the following school year. Applications are submitted through HISD's School Choice office at houstonisd.org/schoolchoice. Popular programs — Carnegie Vanguard, HSPVA, DeBakey High School for Health Professions, and dual-language magnet schools — fill quickly, and many have waitlists. Families new to Houston who arrive after the January deadline cannot enter the magnet lottery until the following fall. Contact the School Choice office directly to confirm current application dates, as the window can shift year to year.

districtFaqs.q: How does STAAR testing affect the HISD school calendar?
districtFaqs.a: Texas assesses students in grades 3–8 and high school through STAAR (State of Texas Assessments of Academic Readiness), administered by the Texas Education Agency each spring — typically April and May. STAAR testing dates are set by TEA and built into the HISD academic calendar. Schools typically suspend standardized field trips and major events during testing weeks to minimize disruptions. High school students take STAAR end-of-course exams in December and May. Specific STAAR testing dates for the current school year are published on the TEA website at tea.texas.gov.

districtFaqs.q: Does HISD observe Veterans Day as a school holiday?
districtFaqs.a: Veterans Day (November 11) is not consistently observed as a full school holiday across HISD — it varies by school year. In some years it falls on a weekend; in others the district may schedule a staff workday or a regular school day around that date. Check the official HISD academic calendar at houstonisd.org each year to confirm whether November 11 is a school holiday or a regular attendance day.

districtFaqs.q: How does HISD's spring break timing compare to Katy ISD and other Harris County suburban districts?
districtFaqs.a: HISD's spring break typically falls in early March — one of the earlier spring break windows among major Texas school districts. Harris County suburban districts including Katy ISD, Cy-Fair ISD, and Spring ISD often have spring breaks in March as well, but the specific weeks do not always align with HISD. Before booking family travel, verify each district's calendar independently. A family with children in HISD and a sibling in Katy ISD, for example, should confirm whether their spring breaks overlap — they frequently do not.

districtFaqs.q: Can I verify which HISD school my child will attend before we move?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. HISD provides a school locator tool at houstonisd.org that uses your exact street address to identify your assigned elementary, middle, and high school. Attendance zone boundaries in Houston run at the street level — two homes in the same zip code or subdivision may be assigned to different schools if a zone boundary runs between them. Always verify school assignment by your specific address, not by zip code or neighborhood name, before committing to a home.

districtFaqs.q: Does HISD provide school bus transportation?
districtFaqs.a: HISD provides bus transportation to eligible students, but eligibility criteria vary — generally, elementary students who live more than two miles from their assigned school and middle and high school students who live more than three miles qualify. Students attending magnet schools outside their neighborhood may also be eligible for transportation depending on the program. The HISD Transportation Department at houstonisd.org/transportation can confirm eligibility for your child's specific school assignment and address.

districtFaqs.q: What is HISD's early release schedule?
districtFaqs.a: HISD schedules early release days throughout the school year for staff professional development, parent-teacher conferences, and similar purposes. These days are published in the official HISD academic calendar and are different from full no-school days — students attend for a shortened day and are dismissed early. Families should add all early release days to their personal calendars separately when the new school year calendar is released, as they require adjusted childcare and pickup arrangements.

districtFaqs.q: How does Texas Education Agency oversight affect HISD?
districtFaqs.a: Since March 2023, HISD has been under Texas Education Agency state conservatorship, following academic performance concerns at Wheatley High School that triggered TEA's intervention authority under state law. A state-appointed superintendent oversees the district in place of the elected school board. Day-to-day school operations continue, and the academic calendar is published on the standard HISD schedule. Families should be aware that administrative structures and some program offerings may evolve during the conservatorship — checking houstonisd.org for current enrollment information is recommended, particularly for magnet program availability.

districtFaqs.q: When does HISD typically start the school year compared to other Texas districts?
districtFaqs.a: HISD typically begins the school year in the first or second week of August — among the earliest start dates of any major urban school district in the United States. Texas state law requires school years to start no earlier than the fourth Monday in August without a waiver, and HISD has historically applied for and received early start waivers. Families relocating from states where school starts after Labor Day should adjust summer childcare, camp, and travel planning to account for an early August start date. Check the official HISD calendar each year for the confirmed first day of school.

districtFaqs.q: Does HISD have UIL athletics, and how does UIL affect the school year calendar?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. HISD schools participate in UIL (University Interscholastic League) athletics, the governing body for Texas public school extracurriculars. UIL playoff schedules for sports like football and basketball extend into November and December for qualifying teams, and UIL academic competitions run through spring. UIL playoff and championship events are not reflected in the standard district academic calendar — families with student-athletes should follow their school's athletic department schedule separately, as UIL events can require travel and missed school days for participating students.

districtFaqs.q: When does kindergarten registration open for HISD?
districtFaqs.a: HISD kindergarten registration typically opens in February or March for the following fall school year. Texas requires children to be five years old by September 1 to enter kindergarten. Documents typically required at registration: certified birth certificate, current Texas immunization record, and two proofs of Houston residency (utility bill, lease, or mortgage statement). Families relocating to Houston in late spring or summer should contact their assigned neighborhood school directly rather than waiting for a formal registration window — popular campuses can fill before summer, and starting the process early avoids placement delays at the start of the school year.

districtFaqs.q: What are typical school hours for HISD campuses?
districtFaqs.a: HISD school hours vary by campus and grade level — the district does not operate all schools on the same bell schedule. Elementary schools generally start between 7:30 and 8:30 AM and dismiss between 2:30 and 3:30 PM. Middle and high school start and dismissal times differ by campus. Check your specific school's bell schedule at houstonisd.org, as times can also shift year to year. For morning commute and after-school childcare planning, confirm the bell schedule directly with your assigned campus before the school year begins.

districtFaqs.q: How do I enroll a child mid-year in HISD?
districtFaqs.a: HISD accepts mid-year enrollment throughout the school year at the neighborhood school assigned to your address. Required documents typically include: proof of age (birth certificate or passport), current immunization records, proof of Houston residency (lease, utility bill, or similar), and academic records from the previous school. HISD will generally accept unofficial transcripts or teacher-provided summaries on a provisional basis while official records are in transit — families relocating from out of state or internationally should bring whatever records are available and request official transfers afterward. Contact your assigned campus directly to schedule an enrollment appointment; walk-in enrollment is not always available.

districtFaqs.q: Does HISD have a gifted and talented program separate from magnet schools?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. HISD operates the VANGUARD program for academically gifted students, which is distinct from the magnet school network. VANGUARD provides a differentiated gifted and talented curriculum at designated campuses across the district, from elementary through high school. Placement requires a formal referral and district assessment process — families should contact their child's current campus or HISD's Gifted and Talented department at houstonisd.org for current testing windows and eligibility criteria. Like magnet programs, VANGUARD seats are limited and testing timelines matter for families who relocate to Houston mid-year.

districtFaqs.q: When are HISD report cards issued?
districtFaqs.a: HISD issues report cards at the end of each grading period, but the number of grading periods and their timing varies by school level. Elementary, middle, and high school campuses may operate on different grading cycles — typically six or nine weeks per period. Report card release dates are published in the HISD academic calendar and communicated by individual campuses at the start of each school year. Check your school's parent communication platform or houstonisd.org for the current grading calendar, as specific dates are not uniform across all HISD campuses.

districtFaqs.q: When does HISD hold high school graduation ceremonies?
districtFaqs.a: HISD high school graduation ceremonies are typically held in late May or early June, in the days following the last day of school. Each HISD high school schedules its own ceremony independently — dates, times, and venues vary by campus. Some ceremonies are held at large off-campus venues that have limited availability and require early booking. Families planning to travel from out of town for graduation should watch for ceremony date announcements from the specific high school, which are typically made in early spring. Check houstonisd.org and your school's website for official graduation information each year.

livingHere.intro: Houston is the energy capital of the world and the most ethnically diverse large city in the United States, anchored by the Texas Medical Center, NASA's Johnson Space Center, and the Port of Houston. For families relocating to the area, a few geographic and climate realities shape how the school calendar intersects with everyday life.

highlights.label: Two Major Airports
highlights.detail: George Bush Intercontinental (IAH) handles most international and long-haul flights; William P. Hobby (HOU) serves Southwest and regional routes. Spring Break flights out of both airports fill within hours of school-year calendars being published.

highlights.label: Hurricane Season
highlights.detail: Runs June–November, with peak risk August–October — overlapping the first weeks of school. Harris County flooding is a known compounding risk; families in low-lying areas should have emergency plans and contact info for HISD's weather closure hotline.

highlights.label: Extreme Summer Heat
highlights.detail: Houston averages over 100°F on multiple days in July and August — the same weeks as back-to-school. After-school outdoor sports practices are limited by UIL heat protocols; hydration and shaded waiting areas at dismissal are practical daily concerns.

highlights.label: Texas Medical Center
highlights.detail: The world's largest medical complex, employing over 100,000 people within Houston. Many HISD families work rotating hospital shifts — the district's early August start date is often out of step with academic medical calendar cycles.

highlights.label: Port of Houston
highlights.detail: One of the busiest cargo ports in the US, driving the region's logistics and petrochemical industries. Spring Break cargo disruptions are uncommon, but the port's workforce makes Houston's economy less seasonal than most Sun Belt metros.

highlights.label: No State Income Tax
highlights.detail: Texas has no personal income tax, which drives significant relocation from California, Illinois, and New York. HISD sees a steady stream of mid-year transfers — families arriving from these states should verify enrollment requirements, as grading systems and graduation requirements differ from their home states.

compareIntro: HISD's school calendar doesn't align with its Harris County suburban neighbors as closely as families often assume. Katy ISD, Cy-Fair, Spring ISD, and Clear Creek ISD each publish independent calendars with their own spring break weeks — and HISD's early August start is shared by some suburban districts but not all. Families coordinating between HISD and a neighboring district for childcare or shared family schedules should verify both calendars each year rather than assuming breaks overlap.

relatedDistricts.name: Katy Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Katy ISD is the most commonly compared Harris County suburban district for families leaving HISD — serving the Katy, Cinco Ranch, and Energy Corridor corridor west of Houston. Spring break dates between HISD and Katy ISD frequently do not align. Families with children enrolled in both districts cannot assume shared break windows without checking each district's calendar.

relatedDistricts.name: Dallas Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Dallas ISD and Houston ISD are Texas's two largest urban school districts, and families frequently relocate between the two metro areas for energy sector, healthcare, and logistics employment. Both districts share early August start dates and large magnet school networks, but their spring break weeks and holiday schedules differ — verify both calendars before planning cross-city family travel.

relatedDistricts.name: Fort Worth Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Fort Worth ISD is a large Texas urban district in the DFW metro, frequently compared by families transferring between Houston and North Texas. Fort Worth ISD's spring break typically falls in a different week than HISD's early March break — families coordinating across both districts should verify each year's calendars separately.

relatedDistricts.name: Conroe Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Conroe ISD is the dominant school district for families living north of Houston in The Woodlands and Montgomery County, and is one of the most common destinations for HISD families relocating out of Harris County along the I-45 corridor. The two districts set calendars independently — Spring Break dates frequently fall in different weeks, and Conroe ISD's first-day timing often differs from HISD's by a day or more. Families with children split across both districts, or coordinating travel with Conroe ISD relatives, should verify each calendar separately each year rather than assuming alignment.
